result:
I’ve been thinking about the strange overlap between maps and stories.

A map is supposed to be an authoritative slice of reality—a polished sheet that flattens mountains, translates winding rivers into precise blue threads, and distills entire cities to careful clusters of symbols. Stories, on the other hand, are all about subjectivity. They thrive on perspective, on the way a single moment feels to one person, or the way a decision echoes down through time. But somewhere in that intersection, where a well-told story can guide you through unfamiliar terrain the way a map does, and a good map can whisper the beginnings of adventure the moment you unfold it—that’s the realm where imagination and orientation collide.

Maybe that’s why I’m fascinated by the “you are here” dot. It’s the simplest bit of cartographic vocabulary, almost an afterthought, yet it changes everything. A map without it is an abstract possibility—beautiful, but detached. Add the dot, and suddenly the paper knows you exist. It acknowledges the present tense and suggests paths: steps to take, roads to walk, the café around the corner you didn’t know was there. Stories work the same way. The moment we recognize ourselves within them—see some echo of our hopes, anxieties, or quirks—the narrative stops being a safe object and becomes a mirror, a prompt, a quiet nudge.

When I imagine sharing a map with someone, I picture two people unfolding paper in a breeze, pointing at ridges or neighborhoods, discovering a shared sense of place. When I imagine telling a story, I picture the same thing—someone leaning in, letting a voice guide them toward new territory. Both acts demand trust. Both involve curiosity. Both ask us to hold a pattern in our minds and then choose a direction.

If you think about it, even a conversation is a map. We’re charting what we know, discovering what we don’t, and leaving breadcrumbs—ideas and phrases that help us return later. We sketch routes through thoughts, mark places worth revisiting, and sometimes we stumble into blank spaces labeled only with that old cartographer’s warning: “Here be dragons.” But the dragons aren’t necessarily dangerous; they’re just the parts we haven’t explored yet.

So, where are you on your map right now? Is the “you are here” dot blinking somewhere expected, or did it land in unexplored territory? Either way, I hope your story unrolls like a trail worth walking—full of detours, full of discoveries, with just enough dragons to keep things interesting.
